skill.name: caveman-commit
skill.description: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
skill.summary: Generates concise, professional commit messages following the Conventional Commits standard.
skill.author: juliusbrussee

security_audit.summary: The skill file (SKILL.md, 65 lines) is a pure Markdown instruction document. It contains no executable code, no scripts, no network requests, no file system operations, and no environment variable access. The static analyzer flagged 21 external_commands instances, but these are references to git commands in markdown prose and examples — the skill explicitly states it does NOT run git commands. The blocker finding for a weak cryptographic algorithm at line 3 is a false positive; line 3 contains YAML frontmatter description text with no cryptography. No prompt injection attempts were detected. The skill is safe for publication.

medium_findings.title: External Commands Referenced in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 21 instances of external_commands. These are git command references in markdown prose and code examples (e.g., 'Does not run git commit' at line 65). The skill explicitly instructs the AI to NOT execute any commands — it only generates formatted text output. All 21 flagged locations are documentation examples, not command execution instructions. Verdict: FALSE POSITIVE.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines are markdown code examples or prose references to git commands. The skill explicitly states 'Does not run git commit' at line 65. No command execution instructions exist in the file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a weak cryptographic algorithm at line 3. Line 3 contains YAML frontmatter description text for the skill. No cryptographic code or algorithms exist anywhere in the 65-line file. Verdict: FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is YAML frontmatter description metadata. No cryptographic functions, libraries, or algorithms are referenced in the entire file.

content.value_statement: Developers spend too much time writing verbose commit messages. This skill generates terse, professional Conventional Commits in under 50 characters while preserving the reasoning behind each change.
actual_capabilities: Generates commit messages in Conventional Commits format with type, scope, and imperative summary
Enforces subject line length limits of 50 characters with a hard cap of 72 characters
Omits commit body when the subject line is self-explanatory
Includes detailed body only for breaking changes, security fixes, data migrations, and reverts
Maintains compressed caveman-style output while preserving technical accuracy
Auto-activates when staged changes are detected in a git repository
limitations: Only generates commit message text — does not stage files or execute git commands
Requires staged changes to be present to produce meaningful commit messages
Does not amend existing commits or interact with git history
Output must be manually copied and pasted into git commit or a git client

use_cases.title: Solo developer rapid commits
use_cases.description: A solo developer working on a side project uses this skill to quickly generate clean, consistent commit messages without breaking their flow. They stage changes and invoke the skill to get a formatted commit ready to paste.

use_cases.title: Team commit message standardization
use_cases.description: A development team adopts Conventional Commits for changelog automation. This skill ensures every team member produces consistent, correctly formatted commit messages regardless of individual writing style.

use_cases.title: Code review commit hygiene
use_cases.description: A senior developer reviewing pull requests uses this skill to rewrite messy commit messages during interactive rebasing. Each commit gets a clear, concise message that explains the why, not just the what.

prompt_templates.title: Basic commit message
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a commit message for my staged changes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick daily use — stage files and ask for a commit message

prompt_templates.title: Scoped feature commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a commit message for a new API endpoint in the users module that adds profile retrieval.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding a specific feature with a clear scope

prompt_templates.title: Breaking change commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a breaking change commit for renaming the orders API endpoint from v1/orders to v1/checkout.
prompt_templates.scenario: Major API change requiring migration documentation

prompt_templates.title: Security fix with auto-clarity
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a commit message for a security patch that fixes an XSS vulnerability in the search input.
prompt_templates.scenario: Security fix where full body context is required for future auditors

output_examples.input: Staged changes: added GET /users/:id/profile endpoint to the API module with mobile-specific response format.
output_examples.output:
feat(api): add GET /users/:id/profile

Mobile client needs profile data without the full user payload to reduce LTE bandwidth on cold-launch screens.

Closes #128

output_examples.input: Staged changes: renamed /v1/orders route to /v1/checkout across all route handlers and documentation.
output_examples.output:
feat(api)!: rename /v1/orders to /v1/checkout

BREAKING CHANGE: clients on /v1/orders must migrate to /v1/checkout before 2026-06-01. Old route returns 410 after that date.

best_practices: Stage only logically related changes before invoking the skill to get an accurate commit message
Review the generated message before using it — verify the type and scope match your project conventions
Use the explicit prompt with context about what changed to get more accurate scope and type selection
anti_patterns: Do not stage unrelated files together — this produces confusing or inaccurate commit messages
Do not use the skill to rewrite commit history during collaborative rebases without team agreement
Do not rely on the skill for security-related commits without manually verifying the body includes sufficient context

faq.question: Does this skill execute git commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill only generates commit message text. It does not stage files, run git commit, or modify your repository. You copy the output and use it manually.

faq.question: What format do the commit messages follow?
faq.answer: Conventional Commits format: type(scope): imperative summary. Supported types include feat, fix, refactor, perf, docs, test, chore, build, ci, style, and revert.

faq.question: When should the commit body be included?
faq.answer: Only when the subject line does not explain the reasoning. Always include body for breaking changes, security fixes, data migrations, and reverts. Skip body for straightforward changes.

faq.question: How do I stop the caveman style and return to normal?
faq.answer: Say stop caveman-commit or normal mode in your prompt. The skill reverts to standard verbose commit message style.

faq.question: Can I customize the subject line length limit?
faq.answer: The skill enforces 50 characters as the target with a hard cap of 72 characters. These are widely accepted git convention limits and should not be changed without team agreement.

faq.question: Which AI tools support this skill?
faq.answer: This skill works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. It can also be distributed through npx skills for 40+ other AI agents including Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, and GitHub Copilot.
